- [TLE/MLE] Output-sensitive segment sweep assumes few reported crossings. Sweep-line intersection is `O((n+k) log n)` for `k` intersections, so dense legal segment sets with `k = Theta(n^2)` still force quadratic event traffic, output storage, or callback work. Dropping late crossings, merging unrelated event buckets, or storing only a capped witness set turns the resource failure into WA.
- [WA] Same-event sweep buckets are linearized by a fixed add/query/delete order. Shared endpoints, equal `x`, equal `y`, equal angle, horizontal-edge contacts, and coincident events can make the equal-key bucket the real decision point, so stable sort order or input order becomes an accidental algorithm.
- [TLE] Dense sweep repair treats one event or active bucket as locally small. Batching equal coordinates, shared endpoints, and overlaps is necessary, but rebuilding all pairwise relations inside a large bucket can be quadratic or worse unless the work is charged to actual reported intersections. A small active-window cap is only a heuristic on coincident or same-line inputs.
- [RE] Mutable sweep comparator keeps a status tree ordered by a moving sweep coordinate. The sweep status is meaningful only for a fixed event position; if an ordered container comparator reads a global coordinate that changes while segments remain inside it, same-coordinate events or swaps can erase the wrong segment or violate ordering invariants. Remove/reinsert or rebuild at event boundaries instead of mutating the comparator underneath the tree.
- [WA/TLE] Robust-predicate-only coverage is treated as complete geometry coverage. Exact orientation or incircle signs are necessary, but they do not define comparator total order, equal-event semantics, hull collinearity policy, polygon validity, witness topology, or arrangement resource bounds; strong adversarial solutions hide in those policy layers.
- [WA/RE] Angle comparator branch cuts are treated as cosmetic tie cases. `atan2`, slope division, cross-only comparison, and half-plane splits without a strict weak order fail on same-ray, opposite-ray, zero-vector, axis, and `-pi`/`pi` wraparound batches; the bad order can then poison hulls, polygonization, sweep events, or visibility witnesses.
- [WA] General-position cleanup drops geometric witnesses that the statement still distinguishes. Deduplicating points, discarding collinear middle points, ignoring zero-length edges, or collapsing tangent and touching contacts may preserve a visually similar picture while changing multiplicity, boundary inclusion, containment, or witness feasibility.
- [WA/RE] Convex-hull degeneracy is handled only as a preprocessing nuisance. Duplicate extremes, all-collinear point sets, non-strict hulls with long flat edges, repeated closing vertices, and collinear boundary chains can make downstream code treat a segment or two-point hull as a strict polygon, or drop boundary points that the task still needs.
- [WA/TLE] Rotating calipers relies on one antipodal advance rule without proving its tie behavior. All-collinear hulls, flat edges, duplicate hull vertices, rectangles, regular polygons, equal support lines, unsorted cycles, and reversed cycles can skip equal optima, loop on ties, or report a pair/width/area that depends on the first accepted tie.
- [TLE] Naive convex-layer peeling recomputes a hull from scratch on every layer. One planar hull by Graham/monotone scan is `O(m log m)`, while convex layers have known `O(n log n)` algorithms; many thin layers make repeated recomputation near-quadratic. A layer cap avoids timeout only by leaving an unproved core heuristic.
- [TLE] Incremental hull update by linear tangent scans assumes few insertions hit the hull. Adding `q` points to an existing hull with linear inside/tangent scans is `O(nq)`, so a stream where most points become extreme gives quadratic work even though static hull construction is near `O(n log n)`. Random insertion order helps average behavior but is not a worst-case certificate.
- [TLE] Naive half-plane intersection uses all pairwise line intersections. There are `O(N^2)` candidate vertices and checking each against `O(N)` constraints gives `O(N^3)`; incremental polygon clipping is still `O(N^2)` before the sorted-deque `O(N log N)` method. Keep cubic or quadratic clipping only behind a proven tiny bound, not as a fallback for dense constraints.
- [TLE/MLE] Incremental clipping growth assumes the maintained feasible boundary stays small. Repeatedly clipping polygons or half-plane regions is simple, but adversarial constraint orders can make the total boundary scan quadratic before the optimized half-plane algorithm is used. Simplifying edges, dropping nearly parallel constraints, or capping active constraints can remove the defining line of a thin feasible region.
- [WA] Polygon contract shortcuts assume convex, simple, hole-free, consistently oriented, and duplicate-free input unless the statement loudly says otherwise. Hidden reflex vertices, horizontal boundary edges, holes, self-touching chains, repeated vertices, overlapping consecutive edges, and vertex-on-edge contacts can all preserve a plausible polygon while changing containment, area, or certificate validity.
- [WA] Polygonization and local repair assume a legal-looking witness family covers the optimum. Hull-first insertion, centroid or radial sorting, local uncrossing, `2-opt`, greedy gap repair, and restart portfolios can end with a valid simple polygon, matching, tour, or visibility structure while missing the only global topology that reaches the required score.
- [MLE/TLE] Full arrangement materialization assumes quadratic features fit. A 2D arrangement of `n` lines has `O(n^2)` vertices, edges, and faces, so a DCEL, face adjacency graph, visibility graph, or all-cell flood over the whole arrangement is only viable for bounds that tolerate quadratic objects. Lazy zone-limited exploration is the resource boundary; arbitrary feature caps create missing-topology WA.
- [TLE/MLE] Segment-arrangement blowups are mistaken for rare geometry noise. Many legal segments can create dense intersections, overlapping subcurves, and large equal-event buckets, so storing every crossing, every split edge, or every face boundary overwhelms memory/time; simplifying or merging near-identical features without the topology proof turns the resource shortcut into WA.
- [TLE/MLE] Spatial grid or uniform-hash buckets assume bounded local density. Uniform subdivision and spatial hashing are average-case accelerators, but documented broad-phase bounds still have `O(n^2)` worst cases; clustered points, long same-line segments, or many objects in one cell or adjacent cells force quadratic exact checks. Multiple grid shifts and bucket caps reduce random misses, but they do not certify adversarial geometry.
- [WA] Integer cross-product overflow is treated as numeric slop instead of a topology change. Orientation, area, squared distance, dot product, and containment predicates can wrap before the sign is read, so large-coordinate small-residual cases select the wrong side, hull edge, event order, or boundary class even when the final reported value would fit.
